name: pyzipper
documentation:
```{image} https://img.shields.io/pypi/v/pyzipper.svg
:alt: Current Version on PyPi
:target: https://pypi.org/project/pyzipper/
```

```{image} https://img.shields.io/pypi/pyversions/pyzipper.svg
:alt: Supported Python Versions
:target: https://pypi.org/project/pyzipper/
```

# pyzipper

A replacement for Python's `zipfile` that can read and write AES encrypted
zip files. Forked from Python 3.7's `zipfile` module, it features the same
`zipfile` API from that time (most notably, lacking support for
`pathlib`-compatible wrappers that were introduced in Python 3.8).

## Installation

```bash
pip install pyzipper
```

## Usage

```python
import pyzipper

secret_password = b'lost art of keeping a secret'

with pyzipper.AESZipFile('new_test.zip',
                         'w',
                         compression=pyzipper.ZIP_LZMA,
                         encryption=pyzipper.WZ_AES) as zf:
    zf.setpassword(secret_password)
    zf.writestr('test.txt', "What ever you do, don't tell anyone!")

with pyzipper.AESZipFile('new_test.zip') as zf:
    zf.setpassword(secret_password)
    my_secrets = zf.read('test.txt')
```

## AES Strength

The strength of the AES encryption can be configure to be 128, 192 or 256 bits.
By default it is 256 bits. Use the `setencryption()` method to specify the
encryption kwargs:

```python
import pyzipper

secret_password = b'lost art of keeping a secret'

with pyzipper.AESZipFile('new_test.zip',
                         'w',
                         compression=pyzipper.ZIP_LZMA) as zf:
    zf.setpassword(secret_password)
    zf.setencryption(pyzipper.WZ_AES, nbits=128)
    zf.writestr('test.txt', "What ever you do, don't tell anyone!")

with pyzipper.AESZipFile('new_test.zip') as zf:
    zf.setpassword(secret_password)
    my_secrets = zf.read('test.txt')
```

## Documentation

Official Python ZipFile documentation is available here: <https://docs.python.org/3/library/zipfile.html>

## Credits

The docs skeleton was created with [Cookiecutter] and the [audreyr/cookiecutter-pypackage] project template.

modules.name: test.support.script_helper
documentation: Common utility functions used by various script execution tests
e.g. test_cmd_line, test_cmd_line_script and test_runpy

functions.name: test.support.script_helper.interpreter_requires_environment
documentation:
Returns True if our sys.executable interpreter requires environment
variables in order to be able to run at all.

This is designed to be used with @unittest.skipIf() to annotate tests
that need to use an assert_python*() function to launch an isolated
mode (-I) or no environment mode (-E) sub-interpreter process.

A normal build & test does not run into this situation but it can happen
when trying to run the standard library test suite from an interpreter that
doesn't have an obvious home with Python's current home finding logic.

Setting PYTHONHOME is one way to get most of the testsuite to run in that
situation.  PYTHONPATH or PYTHONUSERSITE are other common environment
variables that might impact whether or not the interpreter can start.

functions.name: test.support.script_helper.run_python_until_end
documentation: Executing the interpreter in a subprocess

functions.name: test.support.script_helper.assert_python_ok
documentation:
Assert that running the interpreter with `args` and optional environment
variables `env_vars` succeeds (rc == 0) and return a (return code, stdout,
stderr) tuple.

If the __cleanenv keyword is set, env_vars is used as a fresh environment.

Python is started in isolated mode (command line option -I),
except if the __isolated keyword is set to False.

functions.name: test.support.script_helper.assert_python_failure
documentation:
Assert that running the interpreter with `args` and optional environment
variables `env_vars` fails (rc != 0) and return a (return code, stdout,
stderr) tuple.

See assert_python_ok() for more options.

functions.name: test.support.script_helper.spawn_python
documentation:
Run a Python subprocess with the given arguments.

kw is extra keyword args to pass to subprocess.Popen. Returns a Popen
object.

functions.name: test.support.script_helper.kill_python
documentation: Run the given Popen process until completion and return stdout.

classes.name: test.support.script_helper._PythonRunResult

methods.name: test.support.script_helper._PythonRunResult.fail
documentation: Provide helpful details about failed subcommand runs
Helper for reporting Python subprocess run results

modules.name: test.support.testresult
documentation: Test runner and result class for the regression test suite.

classes.name: pyzipper.zipfile_aes.AESZipInfo
documentation: Class with attributes describing each file in the ZIP archive.

modules.name: test.support
documentation: Supporting definitions for the Python regression tests.

functions.name: test.support._ignore_deprecated_imports
documentation:
Context manager to suppress package and module deprecation
warnings when importing them.

If ignore is False, this context manager has no effect.

functions.name: test.support.ignore_warnings
documentation:
Decorator to suppress deprecation warnings.

Use of context managers to hide warnings make diffs
more noisy and tools like 'git blame' less useful.

functions.name: test.support.import_module
documentation:
Import and return the module to be tested, raising SkipTest if
it is not available.

If deprecated is True, any module or package deprecation messages
will be suppressed. If a module is required on a platform but optional for
others, set required_on to an iterable of platform prefixes which will be
compared against sys.platform.

functions.name: test.support._save_and_remove_module
documentation:
Helper function to save and remove a module from sys.modules

Raise ImportError if the module can't be imported.

functions.name: test.support._save_and_block_module
documentation:
Helper function to save and block a module in sys.modules

Return True if the module was in sys.modules, False otherwise.

functions.name: test.support.anticipate_failure
documentation:
Decorator to mark a test that is known to be broken in some cases

Any use of this decorator should have a comment identifying the
associated tracker issue.

functions.name: test.support.load_package_tests
test.support.import_fresh_module
documentation:
Import and return a module, deliberately bypassing sys.modules.

This function imports and returns a fresh copy of the named Python module
by removing the named module from sys.modules before doing the import.
Note that unlike reload, the original module is not affected by
this operation.

*fresh* is an iterable of additional module names that are also removed
from the sys.modules cache before doing the import.

*blocked* is an iterable of module names that are replaced with None
in the module cache during the import to ensure that attempts to import
them raise ImportError.

The named module and any modules named in the *fresh* and *blocked*
parameters are saved before starting the import and then reinserted into
sys.modules when the fresh import is complete.

Module and package deprecation messages are suppressed during this import
if *deprecated* is True.

This function will raise ImportError if the named module cannot be
imported.

functions.name: test.support.get_attribute
documentation: Get an attribute, raising SkipTest if AttributeError is raised.

functions.name: test.support.make_legacy_pyc
documentation:
Move a PEP 3147/488 pyc file to its legacy pyc location.

```
param source

The file system path to the source file.  The source file
does not need to exist, however the PEP 3147/488 pyc file must exist.

return

The file system path to the legacy pyc file.
```

functions.name: test.support.forget
documentation:
'Forget' a module was ever imported.

This removes the module from sys.modules and deletes any PEP 3147/488 or
legacy .pyc files.

functions.name: test.support._is_gui_available
documentation: Check whether a gui is actually available

functions.name: test.support.is_resource_enabled
documentation:
Test whether a resource is enabled.

Known resources are set by regrtest.py.  If not running under regrtest.py,
all resources are assumed enabled unless use_resources has been set.

functions.name: test.support.requires
documentation: Raise ResourceDenied if the specified resource is not available.

functions.name: test.support._requires_unix_version
documentation:
Decorator raising SkipTest if the OS is `sysname` and the version is less
than `min_version`.

For example, @_requires_unix_version('FreeBSD', (7, 2)) raises SkipTest if
the FreeBSD version is less than 7.2.

functions.name: test.support.requires_freebsd_version
documentation:
Decorator raising SkipTest if the OS is FreeBSD and the FreeBSD version is
less than `min_version`.

For example, @requires_freebsd_version(7, 2) raises SkipTest if the FreeBSD
version is less than 7.2.

functions.name: test.support.requires_linux_version
documentation:
Decorator raising SkipTest if the OS is Linux and the Linux version is
less than `min_version`.

For example, @requires_linux_version(2, 6, 32) raises SkipTest if the Linux
version is less than 2.6.32.

functions.name: test.support.requires_mac_ver
documentation:
Decorator raising SkipTest if the OS is Mac OS X and the OS X
version if less than min_version.

For example, @requires_mac_ver(10, 5) raises SkipTest if the OS X version
is lesser than 10.5.

functions.name: test.support.find_unused_port
documentation:
Returns an unused port that should be suitable for binding.  This is
achieved by creating a temporary socket with the same family and type as
the 'sock' parameter (default is AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM), and binding it to
the specified host address (defaults to 0.0.0.0) with the port set to 0,
eliciting an unused ephemeral port from the OS.  The temporary socket is
then closed and deleted, and the ephemeral port is returned.

Either this method or bind_port() should be used for any tests where a
server socket needs to be bound to a particular port for the duration of
the test.  Which one to use depends on whether the calling code is creating
a python socket, or if an unused port needs to be provided in a constructor
or passed to an external program (i.e. the -accept argument to openssl's
s_server mode).  Always prefer bind_port() over find_unused_port() where
possible.  Hard coded ports should *NEVER* be used.  As soon as a server
socket is bound to a hard coded port, the ability to run multiple instances
of the test simultaneously on the same host is compromised, which makes the
test a ticking time bomb in a buildbot environment. On Unix buildbots, this
may simply manifest as a failed test, which can be recovered from without
intervention in most cases, but on Windows, the entire python process can
completely and utterly wedge, requiring someone to log in to the buildbot
and manually kill the affected process.

(This is easy to reproduce on Windows, unfortunately, and can be traced to
the SO_REUSEADDR socket option having different semantics on Windows versus
Unix/Linux.  On Unix, you can't have two AF_INET SOCK_STREAM sockets bind,
listen and then accept connections on identical host/ports.  An EADDRINUSE
OSError will be raised at some point (depending on the platform and
the order bind and listen were called on each socket).

However, on Windows, if SO_REUSEADDR is set on the sockets, no EADDRINUSE
will ever be raised when attempting to bind two identical host/ports. When
accept() is called on each socket, the second caller's process will steal
the port from the first caller, leaving them both in an awkwardly wedged
state where they'll no longer respond to any signals or graceful kills, and
must be forcibly killed via OpenProcess()/TerminateProcess().

The solution on Windows is to use the SO_EXCLUSIVEADDRUSE socket option
instead of SO_REUSEADDR, which effectively affords the same semantics as
SO_REUSEADDR on Unix.  Given the propensity of Unix developers in the Open
Source world compared to Windows ones, this is a common mistake.  A quick
look over OpenSSL's 0.9.8g source shows that they use SO_REUSEADDR when
openssl.exe is called with the 's_server' option, for example. See
http://bugs.python.org/issue2550 for more info.  The following site also
has a very thorough description about the implications of both REUSEADDR
and EXCLUSIVEADDRUSE on Windows:
http://msdn2.microsoft.com/en-us/library/ms740621(VS.85).aspx)

XXX: although this approach is a vast improvement on previous attempts to
elicit unused ports, it rests heavily on the assumption that the ephemeral
port returned to us by the OS won't immediately be dished back out to some
other process when we close and delete our temporary socket but before our
calling code has a chance to bind the returned port.  We can deal with this
issue if/when we come across it.

functions.name: test.support.bind_port
documentation:
Bind the socket to a free port and return the port number.  Relies on
ephemeral ports in order to ensure we are using an unbound port.  This is
important as many tests may be running simultaneously, especially in a
buildbot environment.  This method raises an exception if the sock.family
is AF_INET and sock.type is SOCK_STREAM, *and* the socket has SO_REUSEADDR
or SO_REUSEPORT set on it.  Tests should *never* set these socket options
for TCP/IP sockets.  The only case for setting these options is testing
multicasting via multiple UDP sockets.

Additionally, if the SO_EXCLUSIVEADDRUSE socket option is available (i.e.
on Windows), it will be set on the socket.  This will prevent anyone else
from bind()'ing to our host/port for the duration of the test.

functions.name: test.support.bind_unix_socket
documentation: Bind a unix socket, raising SkipTest if PermissionError is raised.

functions.name: test.support._is_ipv6_enabled
documentation: Check whether IPv6 is enabled on this host.

functions.name: test.support.system_must_validate_cert
documentation: Skip the test on TLS certificate validation failures.

functions.name: test.support.temp_dir
documentation:
Return a context manager that creates a temporary directory.

Arguments:

  path: the directory to create temporarily.  If omitted or None,
    defaults to creating a temporary directory using tempfile.mkdtemp.

  quiet: if False (the default), the context manager raises an exception
    on error.  Otherwise, if the path is specified and cannot be
    created, only a warning is issued.

functions.name: test.support.change_cwd
documentation:
Return a context manager that changes the current working directory.

Arguments:

  path: the directory to use as the temporary current working directory.

  quiet: if False (the default), the context manager raises an exception
    on error.  Otherwise, it issues only a warning and keeps the current
    working directory the same.

functions.name: test.support.temp_cwd
documentation:
Context manager that temporarily creates and changes the CWD.

The function temporarily changes the current working directory
after creating a temporary directory in the current directory with
name *name*.  If *name* is None, the temporary directory is
created using tempfile.mkdtemp.

If *quiet* is False (default) and it is not possible to
create or change the CWD, an error is raised.  If *quiet* is True,
only a warning is raised and the original CWD is used.

functions.name: test.support.temp_umask
documentation: Context manager that temporarily sets the process umask.

functions.name: test.support.findfile
documentation:
Try to find a file on sys.path or in the test directory.  If it is not
found the argument passed to the function is returned (this does not
necessarily signal failure; could still be the legitimate path).

Setting *subdir* indicates a relative path to use to find the file
rather than looking directly in the path directories.

functions.name: test.support.create_empty_file
documentation: Create an empty file. If the file already exists, truncate it.

functions.name: test.support.make_bad_fd
documentation:
Create an invalid file descriptor by opening and closing a file and return
its fd.

functions.name: test.support._filterwarnings
documentation:
Catch the warnings, then check if all the expected
warnings have been raised and re-raise unexpected warnings.
If 'quiet' is True, only re-raise the unexpected warnings.

functions.name: test.support.check_warnings
documentation:
Context manager to silence warnings.

Accept 2-tuples as positional arguments:
    ("message regexp", WarningCategory)

Optional argument:
 - if 'quiet' is True, it does not fail if a filter catches nothing
    (default True without argument,
     default False if some filters are defined)

Without argument, it defaults to:
    check_warnings(("", Warning), quiet=True)

functions.name: test.support.check_no_warnings
documentation:
Context manager to check that no warnings are emitted.

This context manager enables a given warning within its scope
and checks that no warnings are emitted even with that warning
enabled.

If force_gc is True, a garbage collection is attempted before checking
for warnings. This may help to catch warnings emitted when objects
are deleted, such as ResourceWarning.

Other keyword arguments are passed to warnings.filterwarnings().

functions.name: test.support.check_no_resource_warning
documentation:
Context manager to check that no ResourceWarning is emitted.

Usage:

    with check_no_resource_warning(self):
        f = open(...)
        ...
        del f

You must remove the object which may emit ResourceWarning before
the end of the context manager.

functions.name: test.support.transient_internet
documentation:
Return a context manager that raises ResourceDenied when various issues
with the Internet connection manifest themselves as exceptions.

functions.name: test.support.captured_output
documentation:
Return a context manager used by captured_stdout/stdin/stderr
that temporarily replaces the sys stream *stream_name* with a StringIO.

functions.name: test.support.captured_stdout
documentation:
Capture the output of sys.stdout:

with captured_stdout() as stdout:
    print("hello")
self.assertEqual(stdout.getvalue(), "hello\n")

functions.name: test.support.captured_stderr
documentation:
Capture the output of sys.stderr:

with captured_stderr() as stderr:
    print("hello", file=sys.stderr)
self.assertEqual(stderr.getvalue(), "hello\n")

functions.name: test.support.captured_stdin
documentation:
Capture the input to sys.stdin:

with captured_stdin() as stdin:
    stdin.write('hello\n')
    stdin.seek(0)
    # call test code that consumes from sys.stdin
    captured = input()
self.assertEqual(captured, "hello")

functions.name: test.support.gc_collect
documentation:
Force as many objects as possible to be collected.

In non-CPython implementations of Python, this is needed because timely
deallocation is not guaranteed by the garbage collector.  (Even in CPython
this can be the case in case of reference cycles.)  This means that __del__
methods may be called later than expected and weakrefs may remain alive for
longer than expected.  This function tries its best to force all garbage
objects to disappear.

functions.name: test.support.python_is_optimized
documentation: Find if Python was built with optimizations.

functions.name: test.support.run_with_locale
documentation: Decorator for running a function in a different locale, correctly resetting
it afterwards.

functions.name: test.support.run_with_tz
documentation: Decorator for running a function in a specific timezone, correctly
resetting it afterwards.

functions.name: test.support.bigmemtest
documentation:
Decorator for bigmem tests.

'size' is a requested size for the test (in arbitrary, test-interpreted
units.) 'memuse' is the number of bytes per unit for the test, or a good
estimate of it. For example, a test that needs two byte buffers, of 4 GiB
each, could be decorated with @bigmemtest(size=_4G, memuse=2).

The 'size' argument is normally passed to the decorated test method as an
extra argument. If 'dry_run' is true, the value passed to the test method
may be less than the requested value. If 'dry_run' is false, it means the
test doesn't support dummy runs when -M is not specified.

functions.name: test.support.bigaddrspacetest
documentation: Decorator for tests that fill the address space.

functions.name: test.support.cpython_only
documentation: Decorator for tests only applicable on CPython.

functions.name: test.support.check_impl_detail
documentation:
This function returns True or False depending on the host platform.
Examples:
   if check_impl_detail():               # only on CPython (default)
   if check_impl_detail(jython=True):    # only on Jython
   if check_impl_detail(cpython=False):  # everywhere except on CPython
Use the following check to guard CPython's implementation-specific tests --
or to run them only on the implementation(s) guarded by the arguments.

functions.name: test.support.no_tracing
documentation: Decorator to temporarily turn off tracing for the duration of a test.

functions.name: test.support.refcount_test
documentation:
Decorator for tests which involve reference counting.

To start, the decorator does not run the test if is not run by CPython.
After that, any trace function is unset during the test to prevent
unexpected refcounts caused by the trace function.

functions.name: test.support._filter_suite
documentation: Recursively filter test cases in a suite based on a predicate.

functions.name: test.support._run_suite
documentation: Run tests from a unittest.TestSuite-derived class.

functions.name: test.support.run_unittest
documentation: Run tests from unittest.TestCase-derived classes.

functions.name: test.support._check_docstrings
documentation: Just used to check if docstrings are enabled
Check for the presence of docstrings.
Rather than trying to enumerate all the cases where docstrings may be
disabled, we just check for that directly

functions.name: test.support.run_doctest
documentation:
Run doctest on the given module.  Return (#failures, #tests).

If optional argument verbosity is not specified (or is None), pass
support's belief about verbosity on to doctest.  Else doctest's
usual behavior is used (it searches sys.argv for -v).
doctest driver.

functions.name: test.support.modules_setup
documentation: Support for saving and restoring the imported modules.

functions.name: test.support.threading_setup
documentation: NOTE: we use thread._count() rather than threading.enumerate() (or the
moral equivalent thereof) because a threading.Thread object is still alive
until its __bootstrap() method has returned, even after it has been
unregistered from the threading module.
thread._count(), on the other hand, only gets decremented *after* the
__bootstrap() method has returned, which gives us reliable reference counts
at the end of a test run.

functions.name: test.support.reap_threads
documentation:
Use this function when threads are being used.  This will
ensure that the threads are cleaned up even when the test fails.

functions.name: test.support.wait_threads_exit
documentation:
bpo-31234: Context manager to wait until all threads created in the with
statement exit.

Use _thread.count() to check if threads exited. Indirectly, wait until
threads exit the internal t_bootstrap() C function of the _thread module.

threading_setup() and threading_cleanup() are designed to emit a warning
if a test leaves running threads in the background. This context manager
is designed to cleanup threads started by the _thread.start_new_thread()
which doesn't allow to wait for thread exit, whereas thread.Thread has a
join() method.

functions.name: test.support.join_thread
documentation:
Join a thread. Raise an AssertionError if the thread is still alive
after timeout seconds.

functions.name: test.support.reap_children
documentation:
Use this function at the end of test_main() whenever sub-processes
are started.  This will help ensure that no extra children (zombies)
stick around to hog resources and create problems when looking
for refleaks.

functions.name: test.support.swap_attr
documentation:
Temporary swap out an attribute with a new object.

Usage:
    with swap_attr(obj, "attr", 5):
        ...

    This will set obj.attr to 5 for the duration of the with: block,
    restoring the old value at the end of the block. If `attr` doesn't
    exist on `obj`, it will be created and then deleted at the end of the
    block.

    The old value (or None if it doesn't exist) will be assigned to the
    target of the "as" clause, if there is one.

functions.name: test.support.swap_item
documentation:
Temporary swap out an item with a new object.

Usage:
    with swap_item(obj, "item", 5):
        ...

    This will set obj["item"] to 5 for the duration of the with: block,
    restoring the old value at the end of the block. If `item` doesn't
    exist on `obj`, it will be created and then deleted at the end of the
    block.

    The old value (or None if it doesn't exist) will be assigned to the
    target of the "as" clause, if there is one.

functions.name: test.support.strip_python_stderr
documentation:
Strip the stderr of a Python process from potential debug output
emitted by the interpreter.

This will typically be run on the result of the communicate() method
of a subprocess.Popen object.

functions.name: test.support.args_from_interpreter_flags
documentation:
Return a list of command-line arguments reproducing the current
settings in sys.flags and sys.warnoptions.

functions.name: test.support.optim_args_from_interpreter_flags
documentation:
Return a list of command-line arguments reproducing the current
optimization settings in sys.flags.

functions.name: test.support.skip_unless_symlink
documentation: Skip decorator for tests that require functional symlink

functions.name: test.support.skip_unless_xattr
documentation: Skip decorator for tests that require functional extended attributes

functions.name: test.support.skip_unless_bind_unix_socket
documentation: Decorator for tests requiring a functional bind() for unix sockets.

functions.name: test.support.fs_is_case_insensitive
documentation: Detects if the file system for the specified directory is case-insensitive.

functions.name: test.support.detect_api_mismatch
documentation:
Returns the set of items in ref_api not in other_api, except for a
defined list of items to be ignored in this check.

By default this skips private attributes beginning with '_' but
includes all magic methods, i.e. those starting and ending in '__'.

functions.name: test.support.check__all__
documentation:
Assert that the __all__ variable of 'module' contains all public names.

The module's public names (its API) are detected automatically based on
whether they match the public name convention and were defined in
'module'.

The 'name_of_module' argument can specify (as a string or tuple thereof)
what module(s) an API could be defined in in order to be detected as a
public API. One case for this is when 'module' imports part of its public
API from other modules, possibly a C backend (like 'csv' and its '_csv').

The 'extra' argument can be a set of names that wouldn't otherwise be
automatically detected as "public", like objects without a proper
'__module__' attribute. If provided, it will be added to the
automatically detected ones.

The 'blacklist' argument can be a set of names that must not be treated
as part of the public API even though their names indicate otherwise.

Usage:
    import bar
    import foo
    import unittest
    from test import support

    class MiscTestCase(unittest.TestCase):
        def test__all__(self):
            support.check__all__(self, foo)

    class OtherTestCase(unittest.TestCase):
        def test__all__(self):
            extra = {'BAR_CONST', 'FOO_CONST'}
            blacklist = {'baz'}  # Undocumented name.
            # bar imports part of its API from _bar.
            support.check__all__(self, bar, ('bar', '_bar'),
                                 extra=extra, blacklist=blacklist)

functions.name: test.support.patch
documentation:
Override 'object_to_patch'.'attr_name' with 'new_value'.

Also, add a cleanup procedure to 'test_instance' to restore
'object_to_patch' value for 'attr_name'.
The 'attr_name' should be a valid attribute for 'object_to_patch'.

functions.name: test.support.run_in_subinterp
documentation:
Run code in a subinterpreter. Raise unittest.SkipTest if the tracemalloc
module is enabled.

functions.name: test.support.missing_compiler_executable
documentation:
Check if the compiler components used to build the interpreter exist.

Check for the existence of the compiler executables whose names are listed
in 'cmd_names' or all the compiler executables when 'cmd_names' is empty
and return the first missing executable or None when none is found
missing.

functions.name: test.support.fd_count
documentation: Count the number of open file descriptors.

functions.name: test.support.maybe_get_event_loop_policy
documentation: Return the global event loop policy if one is set, else return None.

variables.name: test.support.verbose
documentation: Flag set to 0 by regrtest.py

variables.name: test.support.use_resources
documentation: Flag set to [] by regrtest.py

variables.name: test.support.max_memuse
documentation: Disable bigmem tests (they will still be run with

variables.name: test.support.junit_xml_list
documentation: list of testsuite XML elements

classes.name: test.support.Error
documentation: Base class for regression test exceptions.

classes.name: test.support.TestFailed
documentation: Test failed.

classes.name: test.support.ResourceDenied
documentation:
Test skipped because it requested a disallowed resource.

This is raised when a test calls requires() for a resource that
has not be enabled.  It is used to distinguish between expected
and unexpected skips.

classes.name: test.support.WarningsRecorder
documentation:
Convenience wrapper for the warnings list returned on
entry to the warnings.catch_warnings() context manager.

classes.name: test.support.CleanImport
documentation:
Context manager to force import to return a new module reference.

This is useful for testing module-level behaviours, such as
the emission of a DeprecationWarning on import.

Use like this:

    with CleanImport("foo"):
        importlib.import_module("foo") # new reference

classes.name: test.support.EnvironmentVarGuard
documentation:
Class to help protect the environment variable properly.  Can be used as
a context manager.

classes.name: test.support.DirsOnSysPath
documentation:
Context manager to temporarily add directories to sys.path.

This makes a copy of sys.path, appends any directories given
as positional arguments, then reverts sys.path to the copied
settings when the context ends.

Note that *all* sys.path modifications in the body of the
context manager, including replacement of the object,
will be reverted at the end of the block.

classes.name: test.support.TransientResource

methods.name: test.support.TransientResource.__exit__
documentation:
If type_ is a subclass of self.exc and value has attributes matching
self.attrs, raise ResourceDenied.  Otherwise let the exception
propagate (if any).

Raise ResourceDenied if an exception is raised while the context manager
is in effect that matches the specified exception and attributes.

classes.name: test.support._MemoryWatchdog
documentation:
An object which periodically watches the process' memory consumption
and prints it out.

classes.name: test.support.BasicTestRunner
documentation: unittest integration.

classes.name: test.support.TestHandler

methods.name: test.support.TestHandler.matches
documentation: Look for a saved dict whose keys/values match the supplied arguments.
Support for assertions about logging.

methods.name: test.support.Matcher.matches
documentation:
Try to match a single dict with the supplied arguments.

Keys whose values are strings and which are in self._partial_matches
will be checked for partial (i.e. substring) matches. You can extend
this scheme to (for example) do regular expression matching, etc.

methods.name: test.support.Matcher.match_value
documentation: Try to match a single stored value (dv) with a supplied value (v).

classes.name: test.support.SuppressCrashReport

methods.name: test.support.SuppressCrashReport.__enter__
documentation:
On Windows, disable Windows Error Reporting dialogs using
SetErrorMode.

On UNIX, try to save the previous core file size limit, then set
soft limit to 0.

methods.name: test.support.SuppressCrashReport.__exit__
documentation: Restore Windows ErrorMode or core file behavior to initial value.

Try to prevent a crash report from popping up.

On Windows, don't display the Windows Error Reporting dialog.  On UNIX,
disable the creation of coredump file.

classes.name: test.support.SaveSignals
documentation:
Save and restore signal handlers.

This class is only able to save/restore signal handlers registered
by the Python signal module: see bpo-13285 for "external" signal
handlers.

classes.name: test.support.FakePath
documentation: Simple implementing of the path protocol.

modules.name: pyzipper.zipfile
documentation:
Read and write ZIP files.

XXX references to utf-8 need further investigation.

functions.name: pyzipper.zipfile.is_zipfile
documentation:
Quickly see if a file is a ZIP file by checking the magic number.

The filename argument may be a file or file-like object too.

functions.name: pyzipper.zipfile._EndRecData64
documentation: Read the ZIP64 end-of-archive records and use that to update endrec

functions.name: pyzipper.zipfile._EndRecData
documentation:
Return data from the "End of Central Directory" record, or None.

The data is a list of the nine items in the ZIP "End of central dir"
record followed by a tenth item, the file seek offset of this record.

classes.name: pyzipper.zipfile.LargeZipFile
documentation:
Raised when writing a zipfile, the zipfile requires ZIP64 extensions
and those extensions are disabled.

classes.name: pyzipper.zipfile.ZipInfo

methods.name: pyzipper.zipfile.ZipInfo.is_utf_filename
documentation:
Return True if filenames are encoded in UTF-8.

Bit 11: Language encoding flag (EFS).  If this bit is set, the filename
and comment fields for this file MUST be encoded using UTF-8.

methods.name: pyzipper.zipfile.ZipInfo.use_datadescripter
documentation:
Returns True if datadescripter is in use.

If bit 3 of flags is set, the data descripter is must exist.  It is
byte aligned and immediately follows the last byte of compressed data.

crc-32                          4 bytes
compressed size                 4 bytes
uncompressed size               4 bytes

methods.name: pyzipper.zipfile.ZipInfo.zip64_local_header
documentation:
If zip64 is required, return encoded extra block and other
parameters which may alter the local file header.

The local zip64 entry requires that, if the zip64 block is present, it
must contain both file_size and compress_size. This is different to the
central directory zip64 extra block which requires only fields which
need the extra zip64 size be present in the extra block.

methods.name: pyzipper.zipfile.ZipInfo.FileHeader
documentation: Return the per-file header as a string.

methods.name: pyzipper.zipfile.ZipInfo.from_file
documentation:
Construct an appropriate ZipInfo for a file on the filesystem.

filename should be the path to a file or directory on the filesystem.

arcname is the name which it will have within the archive (by default,
this will be the same as filename, but without a drive letter and with
leading path separators removed).

methods.name: pyzipper.zipfile.ZipInfo.is_dir
documentation: Return True if this archive member is a directory.
Class with attributes describing each file in the ZIP archive.

classes.name: pyzipper.zipfile.CRCZipDecrypter

methods.name: pyzipper.zipfile.CRCZipDecrypter.crc32
documentation: Compute the CRC32 primitive on one byte.

methods.name: pyzipper.zipfile.CRCZipDecrypter.decrypt
documentation: Decrypt a bytes object.

PKWARE Encryption Decrypter

ZIP supports a password-based form of encryption. Even though known
plaintext attacks have been found against it, it is still useful
to be able to get data out of such a file.

Usage:
    zd = CRCZipDecrypter(zinfo, mypwd, encryption_header)
    plain_bytes = zd.decrypt(cypher_bytes)

methods.name: pyzipper.zipfile.LZMADecompressor.__init__
documentation: By itself, this decompressor needs an end of stream marker to know when
the compressed stream has finished. If there is no end of stream marker,
but the zip file length is known, the file can still be processed by
ensuring we only pass data to the length of 'compress_size' to the
decompressor.
There should be a check to make sure either the end of stream marker flag
is set or the 'compress_size' is provided to catch malformed files.
https://sourceforge.net/p/lzmautils/discussion/708858/thread/da2a47a8/
"The raw decoder API works only with streams that have end of
payload/stream marker. The raw stream APIs don't support much else than
what would be valid inside a .xz file.
The .lzma file decoder (lzma_alone_decoder) works with files that have a
known size in the header and no end marker. It's handled as a special
case internally and is not exported to raw decoder API; maybe it should
be."

classes.name: pyzipper.zipfile._Tellable
documentation: Provide the tell method for unseekable stream

classes.name: pyzipper.zipfile.ZipExtFile

methods.name: pyzipper.zipfile.ZipExtFile.process_local_header
documentation:
Read the local header and raise for any errors.

The local header is largely a duplicate of the file's entry in the
central directory. Where it differs, the local header generally
contains less information than the entry in the central directory.

Currently we only use the local header data to check for errors.

methods.name: pyzipper.zipfile.ZipExtFile.readline
documentation:
Read and return a line from the stream.

If limit is specified, at most limit bytes will be read.

methods.name: pyzipper.zipfile.ZipExtFile.peek
documentation: Returns buffered bytes without advancing the position.

methods.name: pyzipper.zipfile.ZipExtFile.read
documentation:
Read and return up to n bytes.

If the argument is omitted, None, or negative, data is read and
returned until EOF is reached.

methods.name: pyzipper.zipfile.ZipExtFile.read1
documentation: Read up to n bytes with at most one read() system call.

File-like object for reading an archive member.

Is returned by ZipFile.open().

Responsible for reading the following parts of a zip file:

    [local file header]
    [encryption header]
    [file data]
    [data descriptor]

For symmetry, the _ZipWriteFile class is responsible for writing the same
sections.

classes.name: pyzipper.zipfile.ZipFile

methods.name: pyzipper.zipfile.ZipFile.__init__
documentation:
Open the ZIP file with mode read 'r', write 'w', exclusive create
'x', or append 'a'.

methods.name: pyzipper.zipfile.ZipFile._RealGetContents
documentation: Read in the table of contents for the ZIP file.

methods.name: pyzipper.zipfile.ZipFile.namelist
documentation: Return a list of file names in the archive.

methods.name: pyzipper.zipfile.ZipFile.infolist
documentation:
Return a list of class ZipInfo instances for files in the
archive.

methods.name: pyzipper.zipfile.ZipFile.printdir
documentation: Print a table of contents for the zip file.

methods.name: pyzipper.zipfile.ZipFile.testzip
documentation: Read all the files and check the CRC.

methods.name: pyzipper.zipfile.ZipFile.getinfo
documentation: Return the instance of ZipInfo given 'name'.

methods.name: pyzipper.zipfile.ZipFile.setpassword
documentation: Set default password for encrypted files.

methods.name: pyzipper.zipfile.ZipFile.comment
documentation: The comment text associated with the ZIP file.

methods.name: pyzipper.zipfile.ZipFile.read
documentation: Return file bytes (as a string) for name.

methods.name: pyzipper.zipfile.ZipFile.open
documentation:
Return file-like object for 'name'.

name is a string for the file name within the ZIP file, or a ZipInfo
object.

mode should be 'r' to read a file already in the ZIP file, or 'w' to
write to a file newly added to the archive.

pwd is the password to encrypt and decrypt files.

When writing, if the file size is not known in advance but may exceed
2 GiB, pass force_zip64 to use the ZIP64 format, which can handle large
files.  If the size is known in advance, it is best to pass a ZipInfo
instance for name, with zinfo.file_size set.

methods.name: pyzipper.zipfile.ZipFile.extract
documentation:
Extract a member from the archive to the current working directory,
using its full name. Its file information is extracted as accurately
as possible. `member` may be a filename or a ZipInfo object. You can
specify a different directory using `path`.

methods.name: pyzipper.zipfile.ZipFile.extractall
documentation:
Extract all members from the archive to the current working
directory. `path` specifies a different directory to extract to.
`members` is optional and must be a subset of the list returned
by namelist().

methods.name: pyzipper.zipfile.ZipFile._sanitize_windows_name
documentation: Replace bad characters and remove trailing dots from parts.

methods.name: pyzipper.zipfile.ZipFile._extract_member
documentation:
Extract the ZipInfo object 'member' to a physical
file on the path targetpath.

methods.name: pyzipper.zipfile.ZipFile._writecheck
documentation: Check for errors before writing a file to the archive.

methods.name: pyzipper.zipfile.ZipFile.write
documentation:
Put the bytes from filename into the archive under the name
arcname.

methods.name: pyzipper.zipfile.ZipFile.writestr
documentation:
Write a file into the archive.  The contents is 'data', which
may be either a 'str' or a 'bytes' instance; if it is a 'str',
it is encoded as UTF-8 first.
'zinfo_or_arcname' is either a ZipInfo instance or
the name of the file in the archive.

methods.name: pyzipper.zipfile.ZipFile.__del__
documentation: Call the "close()" method in case the user forgot.

methods.name: pyzipper.zipfile.ZipFile.close
documentation:
Close the file, and for mode 'w', 'x' and 'a' write the ending
records.

class_variables.name: pyzipper.zipfile.ZipFile.fp
documentation: Set here since __del__ checks it

Class with methods to open, read, write, close, list zip files.

z = ZipFile(file, mode="r", compression=ZIP_STORED, allowZip64=True,
            compresslevel=None)

file: Either the path to the file, or a file-like object.
      If it is a path, the file will be opened and closed by ZipFile.
mode: The mode can be either read 'r', write 'w', exclusive create 'x',
      or append 'a'.
compression: ZIP_STORED (no compression), ZIP_DEFLATED (requires zlib),
             ZIP_BZIP2 (requires bz2) or ZIP_LZMA (requires lzma).
allowZip64: if True ZipFile will create files with ZIP64 extensions when
            needed, otherwise it will raise an exception when this would
            be necessary.
compresslevel: None (default for the given compression type) or an integer
               specifying the level to pass to the compressor.
               When using ZIP_STORED or ZIP_LZMA this keyword has no effect.
               When using ZIP_DEFLATED integers 0 through 9 are accepted.
               When using ZIP_BZIP2 integers 1 through 9 are accepted.

classes.name: pyzipper.zipfile.PyZipFile

methods.name: pyzipper.zipfile.PyZipFile.writepy
documentation:
Add all files from "pathname" to the ZIP archive.

If pathname is a package directory, search the directory and
all package subdirectories recursively for all *.py and enter
the modules into the archive.  If pathname is a plain
directory, listdir *.py and enter all modules.  Else, pathname
must be a Python *.py file and the module will be put into the
archive.  Added modules are always module.pyc.
This method will compile the module.py into module.pyc if
necessary.
If filterfunc(pathname) is given, it is called with every argument.
When it is False, the file or directory is skipped.

methods.name: pyzipper.zipfile.PyZipFile._get_codename
documentation:
Return (filename, archivename) for the path.

Given a module name path, return the correct file path and
archive name, compiling if necessary.  For example, given
/python/lib/string, return (/python/lib/string.pyc, string).
Class to create ZIP archives with Python library files and packages.
